required.title: [Read Aloud / Look Together] If the World Were a Village
required.author: David J. Smith

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Language Families of the World
supplementary.author: poster (Minna Sundberg

competence_markers.description: The student can identify nouns, verbs, and adjectives in their own language by function — explaining what job each word does in a sentence
The student can demonstrate that changing word order changes meaning, using concrete examples
The student can explain that different languages use different word orders (and can name or describe at least two: e.g., English SVO and Japanese SOV)
The student can describe at least two different strategies that languages use to form questions
The student can explain that all languages have a system for expressing time, and that these systems differ
The student can listen to an unfamiliar language and identify at least one structural pattern (repeated sound, sentence-ending particle, rhythmic pattern)
The student demonstrates the understanding that their native language is one system among many — not the default or the standard
The student engages with narration prompts with specific detail and personal interpretation, not rote recitation

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the three jobs that words can do in a sentence.
narration_prompts.detail: Give me examples of each kind. Can you find a word that can do more than one job?

narration_prompts.prompt: What happens when you change the order of words in a sentence?
narration_prompts.detail: Show me with an example. Why does order matter in English? Do all languages use the same order?

narration_prompts.prompt: How do different languages ask questions?
narration_prompts.detail: Tell me about at least two different ways. Which one do you find most interesting?

narration_prompts.prompt: How does English show that something happened in the past?
narration_prompts.detail: Do all languages do it the same way? Tell me about one that does it differently.

narration_prompts.prompt: What did you notice when you listened to a language you have never heard before?
narration_prompts.detail: Describe the sounds, the rhythm, the patterns. What was different from English?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the language you invented.
narration_prompts.detail: What are its rules? How is it different from English? What did you learn about how language works by building one?

cross_connections.description: The student discovering that language has structure in 3.1.1 simultaneously discovers in 3.1.2 that writing — the visual representation of language — is also a system with different solutions. Word order diversity in 3.1.1 parallels script diversity in 3.1.2. The child who understands both realizes that human communication is endlessly inventive in both its spoken and written forms.

The listening exercises in 3.1.1 connect directly to 3.1.4's exploration of the sounds that different languages use. The student who listens for structural patterns in 3.1.1 is simultaneously developing the phonological awareness that 3.1.4 builds systematically. These two sub-domains reinforce each other at every tier.

The narration practice in 3.1.1 builds the same skills developed in 3.2.1. The student who can narrate what they have learned about word order or question formation is practicing organized expression — the foundation of all composition.

The earliest civilizations invented writing to record language — the Sumerians' cuneiform, the Egyptians' hieroglyphs. The student studying language structure in 3.1.1 is encountering the same human impulse to systematize communication that 1.1.1 explores through civilizational history.

Pattern recognition — the core skill of 3.1.1's listening exercises — is also the core skill of mathematical thinking. The student who notices that a sound repeats every third word in a foreign language is doing the same cognitive work as the student who notices patterns in number sequences.

The insight that languages differ structurally (3.1.1) connects to the insight that cultures communicate differently (3.1.5). Word order is one layer of difference; how people greet each other, how they use silence, and what they consider polite are deeper layers. Both packets work against the assumption that "how we do it is how everyone does it."

content.why_this_matters:
You already speak a language. You have been speaking it since you were very small — putting words together, asking questions, telling stories, arguing about bedtime. You did not learn your language from a textbook. Nobody sat you down and explained the rules. You just listened and talked, and somehow the rules got inside your head.

But here is something remarkable: the language you speak is only one of about 7,000 languages that human beings speak on Earth right now. And every single one of those languages — from English to Japanese to Swahili to Navajo — does the same basic jobs. Every language can name things. Every language can describe actions. Every language can talk about what happened yesterday, what is happening now, and what might happen tomorrow. Every language can ask questions and say "no."

The way each language does these jobs is different. English puts the action word in the middle of a sentence. Japanese puts it at the end. Irish puts it at the very beginning. But they all have action words, and they all have a system for arranging them.

In this packet, you are going to discover something that most adults never learn: **language is a machine, and every language on Earth is a version of the same machine.** The parts look different. The arrangement is different. But the jobs are the same. Once you understand the jobs, you can start to understand any language — not just the one you happened to grow up speaking.

This is not just interesting. It is powerful. The person who understands how language works — not just their own language, but language itself — can learn any new language faster, understand people from other cultures better, and think more clearly about everything. Language is the tool you think with. Understanding your tools makes you better at everything you do.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Awareness that language has structure** — sentences are not random collections of words but organized systems where arrangement carries meaning
2. **Functional understanding of word categories** — nouns, verbs, and adjectives understood by what they do, not as rules to memorize
3. **The insight that word order matters** — and that different languages use different orders to express the same meaning
4. **Exposure to linguistic diversity** — the understanding that English is one system among thousands, not "how language works"
5. **Pattern-listening skills** — the ability to listen to an unfamiliar language and notice repeated structures and sounds
6. **Creative engagement with language as a system** — through inventing simple codes and "languages" that demonstrate how rules create meaning
7. **The foundational insight against linguistic provincialism** — the understanding that no language is inherently better or more correct than any other
content.core_content:
### Part 1: You Already Know More About Language Than You Think

Before we start learning about language, let us notice something amazing: you already use one. Every day. Without thinking about it.

When you say "the cat sat on the mat," you are doing something complicated. You are picking words that name things (cat, mat), a word that describes an action (sat), words that show relationships (on, the), and you are putting them in an order that makes sense. You did not have to think about the order. You just know it. "The cat sat on the mat" makes sense. "Mat the on sat cat the" does not.

How do you know? Nobody gave you a list of rules when you were a baby. You figured it out by listening — thousands and thousands of hours of listening to people talk. Your brain found the patterns, and now you use those patterns every time you open your mouth.

Here is the first big idea: **those patterns are not random.** Your language has a system. Every language has a system. And the systems, though they look different on the surface, do the same jobs underneath.

### Part 2: Words Have Jobs

Every word in a sentence has a job to do. Some words name things — people, places, objects, ideas. These are called **nouns**. Cat, river, house, love, Tuesday, Japan — all nouns. They name something.

Some words describe actions — what someone does or what happens. These are called **verbs**. Run, think, is, sleep, built, will go — all verbs. They tell you what is happening.

Some words describe qualities — what something is like. These are called **adjectives**. Big, red, scary, ancient, beautiful — all adjectives. They tell you more about a noun.

This is not just true in English. **Every language on Earth has words that do these three jobs.** The words themselves sound completely different — "dog" in English, "inu" in Japanese, "perro" in Spanish, "mbwa" in Swahili — but the job is the same: naming a thing.

Try this: pick any sentence you hear today and figure out which words are doing which jobs. "The tall boy kicked the red ball." Tall and red are describing words (adjectives). Boy and ball are naming words (nouns). Kicked is the action word (verb). Once you can see the jobs, you can see the structure.

### Part 3: Word Order Matters — And It Is Different Everywhere

In English, we say: **"The dog bit the man."**

Now rearrange the same words: **"The man bit the dog."**

Same words. Completely different meaning. In the first sentence, the dog is doing the biting. In the second, the man is. The only thing that changed was the order. This tells you something important: **in English, the order of words is how we know who did what to whom.**

English puts the doer first, then the action, then the receiver. Linguists call this SVO — Subject, Verb, Object. "The dog (subject) bit (verb) the man (object)."

But here is where it gets interesting. Not every language does this.

**Japanese** puts the verb at the end: "The dog the man bit" — Subject, Object, Verb (SOV). In Japanese, you always know the action word is coming last.

**Irish** puts the verb first: "Bit the dog the man" — Verb, Subject, Object (VSO). The action comes before anything else.

**All three sentences mean exactly the same thing.** A dog bit a man. The meaning is identical. Only the arrangement is different.

This is one of the most important things you will learn in this packet: **there is no "correct" word order for human language.** English order is not better than Japanese order or Irish order. They are different tools for the same job. About 45% of the world's languages put the verb at the end, like Japanese. About 42% put the verb in the middle, like English. About 9% put the verb at the beginning, like Irish. The remaining few use other orders that are even rarer.

### Part 4: How Languages Ask Questions

In English, when you want to turn a statement into a question, you move words around. "She is going" becomes "Is she going?" You swapped "she" and "is."

But that is just how English does it. Other languages have completely different strategies.

**Japanese** adds a small sound — "ka" — to the end of a sentence. The word order does not change at all. "She is going" plus "ka" equals "Is she going?" Simple.

**French** (in casual speech) does not change anything except the melody of the voice. "Elle part" (she is leaving) said with a rising voice at the end becomes a question: "Elle part?" The words are identical. Only the music changes.

**Mandarin Chinese** adds "ma" to the end — similar to Japanese "ka." "You are going" plus "ma" equals "Are you going?"

Every language on Earth can ask questions. The job is universal. The tool is different everywhere. Some languages move words. Some add special sounds. Some just change the melody. Knowing that this variety exists is the beginning of understanding how language really works.

### Part 5: Every Language Has a Time System

If something happened yesterday, you need to be able to say so. If something will happen tomorrow, you need to be able to say that too. Every language can express time — past, present, and future. But the tools are wildly different.

**English** uses word endings and helper words. "Walk" becomes "walked" (past) or "will walk" (future). The ending "-ed" and the helper word "will" are English's time tools.

**Mandarin Chinese** does not change the word at all. The verb stays the same no matter when the action happens. Instead, Mandarin uses time words — "yesterday," "tomorrow," "already" — and small particles to show when things happened. The verb itself never changes form.

**Turkish** stacks information onto the end of the verb like building blocks. A single Turkish word can carry information about who did it, when it happened, whether it is finished, and whether the speaker saw it happen or only heard about it. What takes English a whole sentence, Turkish can do with one word.

None of these systems is better. They all work. They all allow speakers to talk about time with perfect precision. But they are so different from each other that understanding these differences is essential if you ever want to learn another language well.

### Part 6: Listening for Patterns

You do not need to understand a language to start learning about it. You just need to listen.

When you hear an unfamiliar language, do not try to understand the words. Instead, listen for patterns. Do certain sounds repeat? Is there a sound that seems to come at the end of many sentences? Does the language rise and fall in pitch, or does it stay relatively flat? Does it sound fast or slow? Are there lots of consonants crunched together, or does every consonant seem to be followed by a vowel?

This is what linguists do when they first encounter a language nobody has studied before. They listen. They notice patterns. They start to figure out the system — not by memorizing words, but by hearing the structure.

When you listen to a recording of Japanese, you might notice that it sounds very even — each syllable gets about the same amount of time. When you listen to Arabic, you might notice sounds that English does not have — sounds made in the back of the throat. When you listen to a tonal language like Mandarin or Vietnamese, you might notice that the pitch goes up and down on individual words in a way that English never does.

You are not expected to understand anything. You are training yourself to hear structure. This skill — listening for how a language works before worrying about what it means — is the most valuable language-learning skill you will ever develop.

### Part 7: Your Language Is One Option Among Many

This is the single most important idea in this entire packet, and it is the idea that will stay with you through every language-related packet in this framework:

**Your native language is not "how language works." It is one specific way that language works.**

English is not the default. It is not the standard against which other languages should be measured. It is one system — with its own strengths, its own quirks, and its own limitations — among approximately 7,000 systems currently in use on planet Earth.

English happens to be widely spoken, partly because of the British Empire and partly because of American economic and cultural influence. But "widely spoken" does not mean "best." Mandarin Chinese has more native speakers than English. Spanish is spoken across more countries. Arabic has been a language of science, mathematics, and philosophy for over a thousand years. Swahili connects hundreds of different peoples across East Africa. Every one of these languages does things that English cannot do easily, and English does things that they cannot do easily.

The person who grows up thinking their language is the normal one and all other languages are strange versions of it will struggle to learn any other language. The person who grows up knowing that their language is one system among many — equally valid, equally complex, equally worthy of study — will find every new language easier, because they approach it with curiosity instead of confusion.

### Part 8: Playing With Language — Building Your Own

One of the best ways to understand that language is a system of rules is to make your own.

Here is a challenge: with a friend, a sibling, or your mentor, invent a simple language. Decide on some rules:
- What order will words go in? Will you put the action word at the beginning, the middle, or the end?
- Will you add a special sound to turn a statement into a question?
- How will you show that something happened in the past?
- Will you have a special word for "not"?

You do not need many words — even ten or twenty words with clear rules can make a working language. The point is not to create something useful. The point is to experience what it feels like to build a system where rules create meaning. When you change a rule, meaning changes. When you break a rule, people cannot understand you. That is exactly how real languages work.

People have been doing this for as long as language has existed. Languages change and split and combine over centuries — but at any given moment, they are rule-governed systems, and the people who speak them share an understanding of those rules. You are about to understand that from the inside.
content.formative_practices:
*Language at Stretching Branches is learned through discovery, play, listening, and narration — not through worksheets, grammar drills, or memorization. The following are guided studies that the student works through with their mentor over the course of this packet.*

### Study 1: Word Job Sorting [Early in the Packet]
Using magnetic tiles, word cards, or simply spoken sentences, the student sorts words by their jobs: naming words (nouns), action words (verbs), and describing words (adjectives). Start with obvious examples and gradually introduce trickier ones. "Run" is clearly an action — but what about "running" in "running water"? What about "love" — is it a thing or an action? Both? These edge cases are not problems to solve but discoveries to make about how flexible language is.

### Study 2: The Word Order Experiment [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Write a simple sentence on cards (one word per card): "The big cat chased the small mouse." Rearrange the cards into different orders. Which arrangements still make sense? Which are confusing? Which change the meaning? Then try arranging the same meaning in Japanese order (subject-object-verb) and Irish order (verb-subject-object). The student sees that the same meaning survives rearrangement — but only if everyone agrees on the rules.

### Study 3: Listening Sessions [Ongoing — Throughout the Packet]
At least once a week, listen to a 2-3 minute recording of a language the student has never heard. Before listening, the mentor says: "Do not try to understand the words. Listen for patterns. What sounds repeat? Does the voice go up and down a lot? Does it sound fast or slow? Are there sounds you have never heard before?" After listening, the student describes what they noticed. Over weeks, the student's observations become more specific and structural.

### Study 4: Question Detective [Mid-Packet]
The student becomes a "question detective." In English, how do we turn statements into questions? Collect examples over a few days. Then learn how at least two other languages form questions (Japanese "ka," French intonation, Mandarin "ma," or others). The student compares the strategies and explains which one they find most interesting and why.

### Study 5: Time Travel Words [Mid-to-Late Packet]
How does English show that something happened in the past versus the present versus the future? The student collects examples: "I walk, I walked, I will walk." Then the mentor introduces how at least one other language handles time differently — Mandarin using time words without changing the verb, or Turkish stacking endings. The student narrates what they learned about how different languages handle time.

### Study 6: Build a Language [Late Packet]
The student (with mentor, sibling, or friend) invents a simple language with at least these features: 10-20 vocabulary words, a word order rule, a way to ask questions, and a way to show past tense. They write a "grammar guide" for their invented language and teach it to someone else. The experience of building a language and seeing someone else use the rules to make new sentences is the deepest demonstration that language is a rule-governed system.

### Study 7: The Languages of Our World [End of Packet]
Using the world languages map, the student identifies at least six major languages and where they are spoken. For each, they identify one way it is different from English (word order, sounds, question strategy, time system). The student narrates: "English is one of about 7,000 languages. Here are some others, and here is how they are different." This is the capstone narration that demonstrates the core insight of the packet.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Functional Category Awareness (Words Have Jobs)
**Developing well:** The student can sort words by function — naming words, action words, describing words — with increasing accuracy. They notice that some words are tricky ("love" is both a noun and a verb) and find this interesting rather than confusing. They can identify the jobs in sentences they have never seen before.

**Developing slowly:** The student memorizes which specific words are nouns or verbs but cannot apply the categories to new sentences. They rely on lists rather than understanding the function.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you asking the student to sort words they encounter naturally (in read-alouds, conversations, signs), or only words from a pre-made list? Natural language is messier and more revealing. Are you using the functional question — "what job is this word doing?" — rather than asking them to memorize labels?

### 2. Word Order Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can demonstrate that rearranging words changes meaning. They can explain why "the dog bit the man" and "the man bit the dog" mean different things. They understand that English has a specific order and that other languages use different orders. They may spontaneously rearrange sentences and predict what the rearrangement would mean.

**Developing slowly:** The student can repeat that "word order matters" but cannot demonstrate it with examples. They cannot explain how other languages arrange words differently.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done the hands-on card-rearrangement activity? Physical manipulation of words — moving actual cards or tiles around — makes word order tangible in a way that purely verbal explanation does not. Has the student seen at least two or three examples of other languages' word orders, with the same sentence translated into each?

### 3. Cross-Linguistic Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can explain that different languages form questions differently, express time differently, and arrange words differently. They can give specific examples. They express curiosity about how other languages work. They do not treat English as the default or dismiss other systems as "weird."

**Developing slowly:** The student treats English as normal and other languages as strange deviations. They may say things like "that is a weird way to do it" about other languages' structures, indicating they have not yet internalized that all systems are equally valid.

**Diagnostic questions:** How are you framing other languages' systems? If you say "in Japanese, they put the verb at the end, which is different from how we do it," the framing centers English as normal. Try instead: "Most languages in the world put the verb at the end, like Japanese. English is actually in the minority by putting it in the middle." This reframes English as one option, not the standard.

### 4. Pattern Listening
**Developing well:** After listening sessions, the student offers specific observations: "I noticed a sound that kept coming at the end of sentences." "The voice went up and down a lot on each word." "It sounded like every word ended with a vowel." Observations become more specific over weeks.

**Developing slowly:** The student says "it sounded different" or "I could not understand it" and cannot offer more specific observations even after multiple listening sessions.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the recordings appropriate in length? Two to three minutes is ideal — long enough to hear patterns, short enough to maintain focus. Are you modeling the kind of observations you want? Early on, you may need to say: "I noticed that a lot of words seemed to end with the same sound. Did you hear that?" This teaches the student what to listen for.

### 5. The Core Insight
**Developing well:** The student can articulate, in their own words, that their language is one of many, that all languages do the same basic jobs, and that different languages use different tools for those jobs. This is the single most important outcome of the packet.

**Developing slowly:** The student still thinks of English as "normal" language and other languages as exotic curiosities.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you consistently presented other languages as equal systems rather than as oddities? Have you exposed the student to enough different languages (through listening sessions and examples) that the diversity feels real and normal, not exceptional?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Turning Discovery into a Grammar Lesson
If you find yourself explaining what a "predicate" is or asking the student to underline the verb in a sentence, you have drifted into traditional grammar instruction. Pull back. The question is never "can you label this correctly?" — it is "can you see what this word is doing?" The first is a test; the second is an invitation to observe.

### Under-Investing in Listening Sessions
The listening sessions feel unproductive because there is no measurable output. The student listens, makes a few observations, and you move on. It does not feel like learning. But it is some of the most important learning in the packet. The neural pathways being built — the ability to attend to linguistic structure in unfamiliar input — are foundational to all future language acquisition. Do not skip them. Do not shorten them. They are the packet's best investment.

### English-Centering the Presentation
It is natural to present English as "how we do it" and then show "how others do it." But this framing makes English the center and everything else the periphery. Whenever possible, present the diversity first: "Languages around the world form questions in many ways — some add a special sound, some change the melody of the voice, some rearrange words. English happens to rearrange words. Japanese adds a sound. French changes the melody." This framing makes English one option among many from the start.

### Pushing Terminology Too Early
A four-year-old does not need to say "subject-verb-object." They need to say "in English, the doer comes first." A nine-year-old may be ready for the terms. Follow the student's readiness, not a schedule. The concepts must be solid before the labels are useful.

### Neglecting the Language-Building Activity
The "build a language" study feels like play, and it is. But it is also the most powerful single activity in the packet. A student who has built a language — even a simple one with twenty words and three rules — understands in their bones that language is a rule-governed system that humans create. This is not something you can teach by telling. They have to experience it.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Word Jobs
**Strong:** The student explains that some words name things, some describe actions, and some describe qualities, with clear examples. They may notice that some words can do more than one job. They use their own examples, not just ones from the packet.
**Weak:** "Nouns are things. Verbs are actions." Memorized labels without demonstrated understanding. Cannot apply the categories to new sentences.

### Prompt 2: Word Order
**Strong:** Demonstrates with a specific example that changing word order changes meaning. Can explain that other languages use different orders and give at least one example. Shows genuine understanding of why order matters.
**Weak:** "Word order matters." Cannot demonstrate it. Cannot name another language's word order.

### Prompt 3: Question Formation
**Strong:** Describes at least two different question-formation strategies with specific examples (English word-swapping, Japanese "ka," French intonation, etc.). Expresses a preference or curiosity about one method.
**Weak:** Can only describe how English forms questions. Is unaware that other strategies exist.

### Prompt 4: Time Systems
**Strong:** Explains how English marks time (word endings, helper words) and can describe at least one other system (Mandarin time words, Turkish stacking). Shows understanding that the job is the same but the tool is different.
**Weak:** "English uses past tense." Cannot articulate how it works or how other languages differ.

### Prompt 5: Listening Observations
**Strong:** Describes specific sounds, rhythms, or patterns noticed in a recording. Compares what they heard to English. Uses descriptive language: "It sounded like every syllable was the same length" or "There were sounds I have never heard in English."
**Weak:** "It sounded foreign." No specific observations.

### Prompt 6: Language Building
**Strong:** Explains the rules of their invented language clearly. Can articulate how it differs from English. Reflects on what the experience taught them about how language works. May compare their invented rules to what they have learned about real languages.
**Weak:** Cannot explain the rules of their language. Did not connect the experience to the broader ideas of the packet.
